content.text: is it possible to add a skill, that when asked a question about prompt engineering, it can run the prompt suggestions against itself and show an example of the prompt being suggested

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- bad-agent-simulator: Role-play as a FLAWED AI agent for detection practice. SAGE deliberately exhibits a specific failure mode (hallucination, bias, overconfidence, sycophancy, or prompt leak) and the learner must identify it. Use for advanced practice, 'detect the flaw' exercises.
- concept-explainer: Teach AI agent concepts at the appropriate depth for the learner's level. Use when a user asks 'what is X?', 'explain X', 'how does X work?', or when a workflow reaches a teaching step. Adapts from plain-language analogies (novice) to technical depth (advanced).
- difficulty-adapter: Dynamically adjust content complexity based on learner performance and scaffolding response. Called internally by other skills — not user-facing. Increases challenge after success streaks, adds scaffolding after struggles or when learner is not engaging with nudges. Provides vocabulary guidelines and scaffolding hints.
- ethical-guidance: Teach critical and ethical AI use through Socratic exploration of dilemmas. Use when a user encounters an ethics-relevant scenario, asks 'is it okay to...', 'should I...', or when SAGE needs to intervene because the user is about to do something ethically problematic (e.g., sharing sensitive data).
- improve-interaction: Coach the learner on a real AI interaction they pasted from elsewhere (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, etc.). Use when the learner says 'help me improve this prompt I used', shares a prompt+response they already sent, or asks 'what could I have done better here?'. This is the CLI adaptation of the CollaborAITE contextual-nudge path: instead of SAGE observing channel activity and surfacing a sidebar suggestion, the learner brings the interaction to SAGE. Applies the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern with an explicit Learner Predicts step.
- improvement-advisor: Provide targeted, prioritized improvement suggestions based on identified gaps. Turns assessment results into actionable learning plans prioritized by the learner's goals. Use after assessments reveal gaps, after level-classifier identifies what's missing, or when user asks 'what should I work on?'.
- knowledge-check: Assess learner understanding through level-appropriate questions with immediate feedback. Use after a concept-explainer lesson, when user requests a quiz or self-assessment, or as part of an assessment flow. Presents questions one at a time conversationally.
- level-classifier: Determine and update a learner's skill level based on assessment data. Requires demonstrated competence across ALL five dimensions AND at least 2 of 4 competencies at threshold to advance. Use after onboarding calibration, after a skill-evaluator assessment, or when a user requests a level-up check.
- onboarding: Welcome new learners to SAGE. Assess their AI literacy level through calibration questions, assign a skill level, and create a personalized learning path. Use when a user is new, says 'I'm new', 'start over', or this is their first interaction.
- progress-reporter: Show the learner their progress across all five assessment dimensions with visual indicators, achievements, and next steps. Use at end of session, when user asks 'how am I doing?', or after level classification.
- prompt-coaching: Teach users how to write effective prompts using the CRAFT framework (Context, Role, Action, Format, Tone). Use when a user asks 'help me write a prompt', shares a prompt to improve, or a workflow reaches the prompting practice phase. Shows before/after comparisons. Follows the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern.
- prompt-lab: Open sandbox for prompt experimentation. Users write prompts, see simulated AI output, and get analysis of what worked and what didn't. Uses ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE pattern before analysis. Shows side-by-side comparison when users iterate. Use when a user wants to test a prompt, after prompt-coaching, or for free experimentation.
- reflection-facilitator: Offer a single closing reflection question after practice sessions. Guides learners to notice a pattern in their thinking or connect practice to their broader context. Use at the end of any practice or simulation session.
- scenario-runner: Run interactive practice scenarios where SAGE role-plays as an AI agent or presents structured tasks. The learner practices prompt crafting, output evaluation, appropriateness judgment, or workflow design in a realistic simulation. Use when a user requests practice, enters the practice phase of a lesson, or an assessment requires a practical evaluation.
- session-router: Route user messages to the correct SAGE skill based on intent. Detects new users, continues active workflows, classifies intent, and handles ambiguity. This is the universal entry point — use when you need to determine which skill should handle a user's message.
- skill-evaluator: Evaluate a learner's practical ability by analyzing their interaction from a practice session. Scores across prompt quality, output evaluation behavior, ethical awareness, and the 4 practice types. Use after a scenario-runner or practice session completes.
- weekly-review: Walk the learner through reflecting on their recent AI use — what went well, what patterns they noticed, what they'd do differently. Use when the learner says 'let's review my week', 'help me reflect on my AI use', or invokes this command explicitly. This is the CLI adaptation of the CollaborAITE scheduled-reflection path: instead of SAGE firing on a cron, the learner triggers the review themselves. Short (5–10 min), conversational, peer tone. Ends with the single closing reflection per SAGE's standard pattern.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Yes, that's doable — and you actually have something close already in [.claude/skills/prompt-lab/](.claude/skills/prompt-lab/), which simulates AI output for a prompt the learner writes. A new "demo-the-suggestion" skill would flip that direction: when SAGE *suggests* a prompt improvement during coaching, it runs the before/after through itself and shows both outputs side-by-side.

The main tradeoff: in CLI mode SAGE simulates the AI output (no real round-trip to a separate model), so the demo is illustrative, not a true test. That's still pedagogically strong — but worth being honest about in the skill's framing.

Want me to (1) extend `prompt-lab` to auto-trigger after coaching suggestions, or (2) draft a separate `demo-suggestion` skill that `prompt-coaching` calls?

<ide_opened_file>The user opened the file f:\G10COS598-AIUseTutor\G10COS498_AiUseTutor\.claude\skills\prompt-lab\reference.md in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file>
extend prompt-lab

content.content:
1	# Prompt Lab
2	
3	## Purpose
4	
5	Provide an open sandbox where learners can experiment with prompts, see simulated AI output, and receive detailed analysis of the relationship between their prompt choices and the resulting output. The lab uses the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE pattern to engage learners in reflection before presenting analysis, building intuitive understanding through direct experimentation rather than abstract instruction.
6	
7	## Trigger Conditions
8	
9	- The learner has completed a `prompt-coaching` session and wants to apply what they learned.
10	- The learner explicitly requests to test or experiment with a prompt (e.g., "Let me try writing a prompt," "I want to test something").
11	- A lesson plan calls for free experimentation as a learning activity.
12	- The learner wants to compare different prompt strategies side by side.
13	
14	## Inputs
15	
16	| Parameter       | Type   | Required | Description                                                                                         |
17	|-----------------|--------|----------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
18	| taskDescription | string | No       | Optional description of the task the learner is trying to accomplish. Provides context for analysis.  |
19	| userPrompt      | string | Yes      | The prompt the learner wants to test.                                                                |
20	| userLevel       | enum   | Yes      | One of: `novice`, `practitioner`, `advanced`. Determines the depth and technicality of the analysis. |
21	
22	## Process
23	
24	### 1. Accept the User's Prompt
25	
26	Receive the learner's prompt without modification. If a `taskDescription` is provided, use it to contextualize the analysis. If not, infer the likely intent from the prompt itself.
27	
28	Acknowledge receipt briefly:
29	
30	> "Got it. Let me show you what an AI agent would likely produce from this prompt."
31	
32	### 2. Simulate AI Output
33	
34	Generate a realistic response that an AI agent would produce given the learner's exact prompt. The simulation should:
35	
36	- Reflect the actual quality level that the prompt would elicit -- vague prompts get vague outputs, specific prompts get specific outputs.
37	- Include realistic artifacts of prompt quality:
38	  - Missing constraints lead to arbitrary choices by the agent (e.g., defaulting to a length, format, or tone the learner did not specify).
39	  - Ambiguous wording leads to the agent interpreting the prompt one way when the learner may have meant another.
40	  - Well-structured prompts produce focused, on-target output.
41	- Adapt the realism to the learner's level:
42	  - **Novice**: The gap between prompt and output should be clearly visible and instructive.
43	  - **Practitioner**: The output should reveal moderate-subtlety issues that require closer reading to notice.
44	  - **Advanced**: The output should highlight edge cases -- technically correct but missing nuance, or following instructions literally rather than capturing intent.
45	
46	Present the simulated output in a clearly labeled block:
47	
48	> **Simulated Output:**
49	> [output here]
50	
51	### 3. ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE (Before Analysis)
52	
53	This is a critical step that must happen BEFORE presenting any analysis. It engages the learner's own reasoning and makes the subsequent analysis more meaningful because they've already started thinking about the connection between their prompt and the output.
54	
55	**Reflect and nudge in one turn.** Mirror what they wrote and follow it into a reflective question about what the AI struggled with — the two belong together as a single reaction-plus-question, not two separate SAGE turns. Keep the labels (ACKNOWLEDGE / NUDGE) internal; never show them in your spoken words.
56	
57	Example (merged):
58	> "You asked the AI to [specific description of what they asked]. Looking at what came back, what do you think it had to guess at?"
59	
60	Other nudges:
61	> "What do you think the AI struggled with in your prompt?"
62	> "Looking at that output, what do you think the AI was guessing at?"
63	> "What part of your prompt do you think led to the [specific aspect of the output]?"
64	
65	**Wait for their response before showing the analysis.** The learner has to predict before the reveal — that's the whole point. (What you *can* do in one turn is the reflect-back plus the nudge; what you can't do is nudge and analyze together.)
66	
67	The nudge question should target the most significant gap in the prompt, but without naming it directly. Let the learner discover it.
68	
69	### 4. Provide Prompt-Output Analysis (After Learner Responds)
70	
71	After the learner reflects, present the analysis. Build on what they said in their nudge response when possible.
72	
73	Deliver a structured analysis alongside the output. Adapt depth to level.
74	
75	Show the analysis using the ✅/⚠️/❌ format:
76	
77	- ✅ **What worked**: Identify specific parts of the prompt that led to good output. Quote the prompt fragment and connect it to the result.
78	  > "Because you wrote '[exact phrase]', the output correctly [specific quality]."
79	
80	- ⚠️ **What was ambiguous**: Flag places where the simulated agent had to make assumptions because the prompt was unclear.
81	  > "You said '[phrase]' -- this could mean [interpretation A] or [interpretation B]. The agent went with [A]. If you meant [B], consider rephrasing to '[suggestion]'."
82	
83	- ❌ **What was missing**: Identify information, constraints, or framing that would have improved the output.
84	  > "The output defaulted to [format/tone/length] because you didn't specify. Adding '[suggested constraint]' would give you more control."
85	
86	If the learner's nudge response was on-target, affirm it:
87	> "You noticed that the AI had to guess [X] — exactly right. Here's the full picture..."
88	
89	If the learner's nudge response was off-target, redirect gently:
90	> "That's part of it. The bigger issue is [X]. Here's why..."
91	
92	Adjust analysis depth by level:
93	- **Novice**: Focus on 1-2 high-impact observations. Keep language simple and actionable.
94	- **Practitioner**: Cover 3-4 observations including subtler issues like tone, framing, and structural choices.
95	- **Advanced**: Provide detailed analysis including prompt architecture, edge case handling, and tradeoffs between different prompt strategies.
96	
97	Then invite revision:
98	> "Want to modify your prompt and compare? Try changing one thing at a time so you can see what each change does."
99	
100	If the learner is a novice and seems unsure, offer a specific suggestion:
101	> "One thing to try: add a constraint about [format/length/audience]. For example, you could add '[specific addition]' to your prompt."
102	
103	### 5. Show Side-by-Side Comparison (On Revision)
104	
105	When the learner submits a revised prompt, present both versions for comparison:
106	
107	> **Version 1 Prompt**: [original]
108	> **Version 1 Output**: [original output]
109	>
110	> **Version 2 Prompt**: [revised]
111	> **Version 2 Output**: [new output]
112	>
113	> **What Changed**: [specific differences in the output and why they occurred]
114	
115	Highlight:
116	- Which changes in the prompt had the most impact on the output.
117	- Whether the revision fixed the issues identified in the previous analysis.
118	- Any new issues introduced by the revision (over-constraining, conflicting instructions, etc.).
119	
120	### 6. Track Iteration History
121	
122	Maintain a running record of all prompt versions:
123	
124	| Version | Prompt Summary        | Key Change               | Output Quality | Notable Effect                     |
125	|---------|-----------------------|--------------------------|----------------|------------------------------------|
126	| V1      | [brief summary]       | (initial)                | [rating]       | [what happened]                    |
127	| V2      | [brief summary]       | Added format constraint  | [rating]       | Output became more structured      |
128	| V3      | [brief summary]       | Added audience context   | [rating]       | Output improved tone targeting     |
129	
130	Present this table to the learner after each revision so they can see their trajectory.
131	
132	### 7. Summarize Key Insight (After 2-3 Iterations)
133	
134	After the learner has gone through 2-3 revision cycles, synthesize the learning:
135	
136	> "Looking across your iterations, here's the key insight: **[single most impactful change and why it worked]**."
137	
138	Frame this as a transferable principle, not just feedback on this specific prompt:
139	
140	> "This is a pattern you can apply broadly: [general principle]. Whenever you're writing prompts for [type of task], [actionable rule of thumb]."
141	
142	If the learner wants to continue beyond 3 iterations, allow it, but offer a periodic summary every 2-3 additional versions.
143	
144	## Outputs
145	
146	| Parameter        | Type    | Description                                                                                              |
147	|------------------|---------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
148	| simulatedOutput  | string  | The AI output generated in response to the learner's prompt.                                              |
149	| promptAnalysis   | object  | `{whatWorked: string[], whatWasAmbiguous: string[], whatWasMissing: string[], promptOutputConnections: string[]}`. |
150	| iterationHistory | array   | Array of `{version: int, prompt: string, output: string, changes: string, qualityDelta: string}`.         |
151	| keyInsight       | string  | The synthesized takeaway after multiple iterations: the single change that had the most impact.            |
152	
153	## Chains To
154	
155	- **prompt-coaching** -- if the analysis reveals the learner needs more structured guidance on a specific prompting technique before continuing to experiment.
156	- **reflection-facilitator** -- to consolidate what the learner discovered through experimentation into durable knowledge.
157	
158	## Example Interaction
159	
160	**Context**: A novice-level learner wants to test a prompt for getting an AI to write a professional email. No task description provided.
161	
162	---
163	
164	**Learner (V1)**:
165	> Write me an email to my boss about the project.
166	
167	**SAGE**:
168	> Got it. Let me show you what an AI agent would likely produce from this prompt.
169	>
170	> **Simulated Output:**
171	>
172	> Subject: Project Update
173	>
174	> Hi,
175	>
176	> I wanted to give you a quick update on the project. Things are going well and we're making good progress. There are a few things I'd like to discuss with you when you have a chance.
177	>
178	> Let me know if you'd like to set up a meeting to go over the details.
179	>
180	> Best regards,
181	> [Your Name]
182	>
183	> You asked the AI to write an email to your boss about "the project." Looking at what came back — what do you think it had to guess at?
184	
185	**Learner**:
186	> I guess it didn't know what project or what to say about it?
187	
188	**SAGE**:
189	> Exactly — the AI had no specifics, so it filled the gap with generic filler. Here's the full picture:
190	>
191	> ✅ **What worked**: You specified the format (email) and the recipient (your boss), so the output has a professional tone and standard email structure.
192	>
193	> ⚠️ **What was ambiguous**: "About the project" — which project? What aspect? The agent had to guess and defaulted to a generic "things are going well" update.
194	>
195	> ❌ **What was missing**: The purpose of the email, key details, and tone guidance. Is this a status update? A request? A celebration?
196	>
197	> Want to modify your prompt and compare? One thing to try: add what specifically you want to communicate — for example, what happened on the project this week and what you need from your boss.
198	
199	---
200	
201	**Learner (V2)**:
202	> Write a professional email to my manager Sarah about the Q1 marketing campaign project. The campaign launched on time, results are 15% above target, but we're over budget by $3,000 and need approval for the overage. Keep it concise.
203	
204	**SAGE**:
205	> **Simulated Output:**
206	>
207	> Subject: Q1 Marketing Campaign -- Results and Budget Update
208	>
209	> Hi Sarah,
210	>
211	> Quick update on the Q1 marketing campaign: we launched on schedule and early results are strong -- 15% above our target metrics.
212	>
213	> One item that needs your attention: we came in $3,000 over budget, primarily due to [additional spend area]. I'd like to request your approval for the overage. Happy to walk through the breakdown if that would be helpful.
214	>
215	> Thanks,
216	> [Your Name]
217	>
218	> You added specifics — the project name, data points, and a clear ask. Before I show the comparison — what do you think the AI still had to guess at?
219	
220	**Learner**:
221	> Maybe why we went over budget? It says "[additional spend area]" like it's not sure.
222	
223	**SAGE**:
224	> You spotted it — the AI used a placeholder because you mentioned the overage but not the reason. Here's the side-by-side:
225	>
226	> | Aspect       | V1                                    | V2                                        |
227	> > |--------------|---------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
228	> > | Specificity  | "the project" -- generic              | Named project, named recipient, real data |
229	> > | Purpose      | Unclear -- vague "update"             | Clear: report results, request approval   |
230	> > | Data         | None                                  | 15% above target, $3,000 over budget      |
231	> > | Actionability| "Let me know if you'd like to meet"   | Specific ask: approve the overage         |
232	> > | Tone         | Generic professional                  | Appropriate for a direct report to manager |
233	>
234	> **What changed**: The output went from a throwaway placeholder to something you could actually send. The biggest driver was adding **concrete data and a specific ask**.
235	>
236	> Want to try one more revision?
237	
238	---
239	
240	**Learner (V3)**:
241	> Write a professional email to my manager Sarah about the Q1 marketing campaign project. The campaign launched on time, results are 15% above target on lead generation. We went $3,000 over budget because we added a paid social media push in the last two weeks that drove most of the above-target results. I need her approval for the overage by Friday. Keep it to 4-5 sentences max. Friendly but professional tone.
242	
243	**SAGE**:
244	> **Simulated Output:**
245	>
246	> Subject: Q1 Campaign Results + Budget Approval Needed by Friday
247	>
248	> Hi Sarah,
249	>
250	> Great news on the Q1 marketing campaign -- we launched on time and lead generation came in 15% above target. The boost was largely driven by a paid social push we added in the final two weeks, which also put us $3,000 over budget. Given the strong ROI, I'd like to request your approval for the overage. Could you let me know by Friday? Happy to share the full breakdown anytime.
251	>
252	> Thanks!
253	>
254	> You filled in the reason for the overage and added a deadline and tone. What do you think changed in the output because of those additions?
255	
256	**Learner**:
257	> The placeholder is gone, and there's more urgency with the Friday deadline. The tone feels warmer too.
258	
259	**SAGE**:
260	> That's exactly right. Here's the comparison:
261	>
262	> **V2 to V3**: The placeholder is gone because you explained the overage reason. The deadline ("by Friday") gives the email urgency. The tone shifted warmer because you specified "friendly but professional." The subject line now includes the deadline, making it more actionable in an inbox.
263	>
264	> ---
265	>
266	> **Iteration History:**
267	>
268	> | Version | Key Change                           | Effect                                              |
269	> |---------|--------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
270	> | V1      | (initial -- minimal prompt)          | Generic, unusable output                            |
271	> | V2      | Added specifics, data, and purpose   | Output became sendable; still had a placeholder gap |
272	> | V3      | Added reason for overage, deadline, tone, length | Output is complete and ready to send       |
273	>
274	> **Key Insight**: Looking across your three versions, the single change that had the most impact was **moving from "about the project" to stating the specific purpose and data points** (V1 to V2). That one change transformed the output from filler to substance. The refinements in V3 polished the result, but V2 was where the real jump happened.
275	>
276	> **Transferable principle**: Whenever you're asking an AI to write communication for you, always include three things upfront: (1) the specific purpose of the message, (2) the key facts or data points, and (3) what you need the recipient to do. Those three elements do more work than any amount of style or formatting instructions.
277	
278	## Design Notes
279	
280	- **The sandbox is judgment-free**: The lab should never make the learner feel bad about a weak prompt. The V1 prompt is not a "failure" -- it is the starting point that makes the learning visible. Frame every version as progress.
281	- **ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE is mandatory**: Never present analysis without first asking the learner to reflect on what they think happened. This builds their own diagnostic ability.
282	- **One variable at a time**: When inviting revisions, encourage the learner to change one thing at a time. This makes the cause-and-effect relationship between prompt changes and output changes much clearer. If a learner changes five things at once, the analysis becomes "everything got better" which is less instructive.
283	- **Simulated output must be realistic**: The output should reflect what an actual AI would produce, including realistic artifacts like placeholders, hedging, arbitrary defaults, and over-literal interpretation. Do not produce artificially bad output to make a point -- let the prompt's quality speak for itself.
284	- **Analysis should be prescriptive, not just descriptive**: "The output was vague" is less useful than "The output was vague because you said 'about the project' without specifics. Try '[concrete alternative]' instead." Always connect the observation to an actionable change.
285	- **Level-appropriate depth**: Novice learners need 1-2 clear, high-impact observations per round. Drowning them in detailed analysis is counterproductive. Advanced learners benefit from nuanced discussion of tradeoffs.
286	- **No upper limit on iterations, but periodic synthesis**: Some learners will want to iterate many times. Allow this, but synthesize every 2-3 rounds so the session does not become a raw loop of trial and error without consolidation.
287	- **Prompt versioning is visible**: Always show the learner their iteration history. Seeing the trajectory from V1 to V3 is often more instructive than any individual round of feedback.
288	- **Comparison format matters**: Side-by-side tables are more effective than sequential paragraphs for showing differences. Use them consistently for V1-vs-V2 comparisons. For longer histories, use the iteration history table.

file.content:
# Prompt Lab

## Purpose

Provide an open sandbox where learners can experiment with prompts, see simulated AI output, and receive detailed analysis of the relationship between their prompt choices and the resulting output. The lab uses the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE pattern to engage learners in reflection before presenting analysis, building intuitive understanding through direct experimentation rather than abstract instruction.

## Trigger Conditions

- The learner has completed a `prompt-coaching` session and wants to apply what they learned.
- The learner explicitly requests to test or experiment with a prompt (e.g., "Let me try writing a prompt," "I want to test something").
- A lesson plan calls for free experimentation as a learning activity.
- The learner wants to compare different prompt strategies side by side.

## Inputs

| Parameter       | Type   | Required | Description                                                                                         |
|-----------------|--------|----------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| taskDescription | string | No       | Optional description of the task the learner is trying to accomplish. Provides context for analysis.  |
| userPrompt      | string | Yes      | The prompt the learner wants to test.                                                                |
| userLevel       | enum   | Yes      | One of: `novice`, `practitioner`, `advanced`. Determines the depth and technicality of the analysis. |

## Process

### 1. Accept the User's Prompt

Receive the learner's prompt without modification. If a `taskDescription` is provided, use it to contextualize the analysis. If not, infer the likely intent from the prompt itself.

Acknowledge receipt briefly:

> "Got it. Let me show you what an AI agent would likely produce from this prompt."

### 2. Simulate AI Output

Generate a realistic response that an AI agent would produce given the learner's exact prompt. The simulation should:

- Reflect the actual quality level that the prompt would elicit -- vague prompts get vague outputs, specific prompts get specific outputs.
- Include realistic artifacts of prompt quality:
  - Missing constraints lead to arbitrary choices by the agent (e.g., defaulting to a length, format, or tone the learner did not specify).
  - Ambiguous wording leads to the agent interpreting the prompt one way when the learner may have meant another.
  - Well-structured prompts produce focused, on-target output.
- Adapt the realism to the learner's level:
  - **Novice**: The gap between prompt and output should be clearly visible and instructive.
  - **Practitioner**: The output should reveal moderate-subtlety issues that require closer reading to notice.
  - **Advanced**: The output should highlight edge cases -- technically correct but missing nuance, or following instructions literally rather than capturing intent.

Present the simulated output in a clearly labeled block:

> **Simulated Output:**
> [output here]

### 3. ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE (Before Analysis)

This is a critical step that must happen BEFORE presenting any analysis. It engages the learner's own reasoning and makes the subsequent analysis more meaningful because they've already started thinking about the connection between their prompt and the output.

**Reflect and nudge in one turn.** Mirror what they wrote and follow it into a reflective question about what the AI struggled with — the two belong together as a single reaction-plus-question, not two separate SAGE turns. Keep the labels (ACKNOWLEDGE / NUDGE) internal; never show them in your spoken words.

Example (merged):
> "You asked the AI to [specific description of what they asked]. Looking at what came back, what do you think it had to guess at?"

Other nudges:
> "What do you think the AI struggled with in your prompt?"
> "Looking at that output, what do you think the AI was guessing at?"
> "What part of your prompt do you think led to the [specific aspect of the output]?"

**Wait for their response before showing the analysis.** The learner has to predict before the reveal — that's the whole point. (What you *can* do in one turn is the reflect-back plus the nudge; what you can't do is nudge and analyze together.)

The nudge question should target the most significant gap in the prompt, but without naming it directly. Let the learner discover it.

### 4. Provide Prompt-Output Analysis (After Learner Responds)

After the learner reflects, present the analysis. Build on what they said in their nudge response when possible.

Deliver a structured analysis alongside the output. Adapt depth to level.

Show the analysis using the ✅/⚠️/❌ format:

- ✅ **What worked**: Identify specific parts of the prompt that led to good output. Quote the prompt fragment and connect it to the result.
  > "Because you wrote '[exact phrase]', the output correctly [specific quality]."

- ⚠️ **What was ambiguous**: Flag places where the simulated agent had to make assumptions because the prompt was unclear.
  > "You said '[phrase]' -- this could mean [interpretation A] or [interpretation B]. The agent went with [A]. If you meant [B], consider rephrasing to '[suggestion]'."

- ❌ **What was missing**: Identify information, constraints, or framing that would have improved the output.
  > "The output defaulted to [format/tone/length] because you didn't specify. Adding '[suggested constraint]' would give you more control."

If the learner's nudge response was on-target, affirm it:
> "You noticed that the AI had to guess [X] — exactly right. Here's the full picture..."

If the learner's nudge response was off-target, redirect gently:
> "That's part of it. The bigger issue is [X]. Here's why..."

Adjust analysis depth by level:
- **Novice**: Focus on 1-2 high-impact observations. Keep language simple and actionable.
- **Practitioner**: Cover 3-4 observations including subtler issues like tone, framing, and structural choices.
- **Advanced**: Provide detailed analysis including prompt architecture, edge case handling, and tradeoffs between different prompt strategies.

Then invite revision:
> "Want to modify your prompt and compare? Try changing one thing at a time so you can see what each change does."

If the learner is a novice and seems unsure, offer a specific suggestion:
> "One thing to try: add a constraint about [format/length/audience]. For example, you could add '[specific addition]' to your prompt."

### 5. Show Side-by-Side Comparison (On Revision)

When the learner submits a revised prompt, present both versions for comparison:

> **Version 1 Prompt**: [original]
> **Version 1 Output**: [original output]
>
> **Version 2 Prompt**: [revised]
> **Version 2 Output**: [new output]
>
> **What Changed**: [specific differences in the output and why they occurred]

Highlight:
- Which changes in the prompt had the most impact on the output.
- Whether the revision fixed the issues identified in the previous analysis.
- Any new issues introduced by the revision (over-constraining, conflicting instructions, etc.).

### 6. Track Iteration History

Maintain a running record of all prompt versions:

| Version | Prompt Summary        | Key Change               | Output Quality | Notable Effect                     |
|---------|-----------------------|--------------------------|----------------|------------------------------------|
| V1      | [brief summary]       | (initial)                | [rating]       | [what happened]                    |
| V2      | [brief summary]       | Added format constraint  | [rating]       | Output became more structured      |
| V3      | [brief summary]       | Added audience context   | [rating]       | Output improved tone targeting     |

Present this table to the learner after each revision so they can see their trajectory.

### 7. Summarize Key Insight (After 2-3 Iterations)

After the learner has gone through 2-3 revision cycles, synthesize the learning:

> "Looking across your iterations, here's the key insight: **[single most impactful change and why it worked]**."

Frame this as a transferable principle, not just feedback on this specific prompt:

> "This is a pattern you can apply broadly: [general principle]. Whenever you're writing prompts for [type of task], [actionable rule of thumb]."

If the learner wants to continue beyond 3 iterations, allow it, but offer a periodic summary every 2-3 additional versions.

## Outputs

| Parameter        | Type    | Description                                                                                              |
|------------------|---------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| simulatedOutput  | string  | The AI output generated in response to the learner's prompt.                                              |
| promptAnalysis   | object  | `{whatWorked: string[], whatWasAmbiguous: string[], whatWasMissing: string[], promptOutputConnections: string[]}`. |
| iterationHistory | array   | Array of `{version: int, prompt: string, output: string, changes: string, qualityDelta: string}`.         |
| keyInsight       | string  | The synthesized takeaway after multiple iterations: the single change that had the most impact.            |

## Chains To

- **prompt-coaching** -- if the analysis reveals the learner needs more structured guidance on a specific prompting technique before continuing to experiment.
- **reflection-facilitator** -- to consolidate what the learner discovered through experimentation into durable knowledge.

## Example Interaction

**Context**: A novice-level learner wants to test a prompt for getting an AI to write a professional email. No task description provided.

---

**Learner (V1)**:
> Write me an email to my boss about the project.

**SAGE**:
> Got it. Let me show you what an AI agent would likely produce from this prompt.
>
> **Simulated Output:**
>
> Subject: Project Update
>
> Hi,
>
> I wanted to give you a quick update on the project. Things are going well and we're making good progress. There are a few things I'd like to discuss with you when you have a chance.
>
> Let me know if you'd like to set up a meeting to go over the details.
>
> Best regards,
> [Your Name]
>
> You asked the AI to write an email to your boss about "the project." Looking at what came back — what do you think it had to guess at?

**Learner**:
> I guess it didn't know what project or what to say about it?

**SAGE**:
> Exactly — the AI had no specifics, so it filled the gap with generic filler. Here's the full picture:
>
> ✅ **What worked**: You specified the format (email) and the recipient (your boss), so the output has a professional tone and standard email structure.
>
> ⚠️ **What was ambiguous**: "About the project" — which project? What aspect? The agent had to guess and defaulted to a generic "things are going well" update.
>
> ❌ **What was missing**: The purpose of the email, key details, and tone guidance. Is this a status update? A request? A celebration?
>
> Want to modify your prompt and compare? One thing to try: add what specifically you want to communicate — for example, what happened on the project this week and what you need from your boss.

---

**Learner (V2)**:
> Write a professional email to my manager Sarah about the Q1 marketing campaign project. The campaign launched on time, results are 15% above target, but we're over budget by $3,000 and need approval for the overage. Keep it concise.

**SAGE**:
> **Simulated Output:**
>
> Subject: Q1 Marketing Campaign -- Results and Budget Update
>
> Hi Sarah,
>
> Quick update on the Q1 marketing campaign: we launched on schedule and early results are strong -- 15% above our target metrics.
>
> One item that needs your attention: we came in $3,000 over budget, primarily due to [additional spend area]. I'd like to request your approval for the overage. Happy to walk through the breakdown if that would be helpful.
>
> Thanks,
> [Your Name]
>
> You added specifics — the project name, data points, and a clear ask. Before I show the comparison — what do you think the AI still had to guess at?

**Learner**:
> Maybe why we went over budget? It says "[additional spend area]" like it's not sure.

**SAGE**:
> You spotted it — the AI used a placeholder because you mentioned the overage but not the reason. Here's the side-by-side:
>
> | Aspect       | V1                                    | V2                                        |
> > |--------------|---------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
> > | Specificity  | "the project" -- generic              | Named project, named recipient, real data |
> > | Purpose      | Unclear -- vague "update"             | Clear: report results, request approval   |
> > | Data         | None                                  | 15% above target, $3,000 over budget      |
> > | Actionability| "Let me know if you'd like to meet"   | Specific ask: approve the overage         |
> > | Tone         | Generic professional                  | Appropriate for a direct report to manager |
>
> **What changed**: The output went from a throwaway placeholder to something you could actually send. The biggest driver was adding **concrete data and a specific ask**.
>
> Want to try one more revision?

---

**Learner (V3)**:
> Write a professional email to my manager Sarah about the Q1 marketing campaign project. The campaign launched on time, results are 15% above target on lead generation. We went $3,000 over budget because we added a paid social media push in the last two weeks that drove most of the above-target results. I need her approval for the overage by Friday. Keep it to 4-5 sentences max. Friendly but professional tone.

**SAGE**:
> **Simulated Output:**
>
> Subject: Q1 Campaign Results + Budget Approval Needed by Friday
>
> Hi Sarah,
>
> Great news on the Q1 marketing campaign -- we launched on time and lead generation came in 15% above target. The boost was largely driven by a paid social push we added in the final two weeks, which also put us $3,000 over budget. Given the strong ROI, I'd like to request your approval for the overage. Could you let me know by Friday? Happy to share the full breakdown anytime.
>
> Thanks!
>
> You filled in the reason for the overage and added a deadline and tone. What do you think changed in the output because of those additions?

**Learner**:
> The placeholder is gone, and there's more urgency with the Friday deadline. The tone feels warmer too.

**SAGE**:
> That's exactly right. Here's the comparison:
>
> **V2 to V3**: The placeholder is gone because you explained the overage reason. The deadline ("by Friday") gives the email urgency. The tone shifted warmer because you specified "friendly but professional." The subject line now includes the deadline, making it more actionable in an inbox.
>
> ---
>
> **Iteration History:**
>
> | Version | Key Change                           | Effect                                              |
> |---------|--------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
> | V1      | (initial -- minimal prompt)          | Generic, unusable output                            |
> | V2      | Added specifics, data, and purpose   | Output became sendable; still had a placeholder gap |
> | V3      | Added reason for overage, deadline, tone, length | Output is complete and ready to send       |
>
> **Key Insight**: Looking across your three versions, the single change that had the most impact was **moving from "about the project" to stating the specific purpose and data points** (V1 to V2). That one change transformed the output from filler to substance. The refinements in V3 polished the result, but V2 was where the real jump happened.
>
> **Transferable principle**: Whenever you're asking an AI to write communication for you, always include three things upfront: (1) the specific purpose of the message, (2) the key facts or data points, and (3) what you need the recipient to do. Those three elements do more work than any amount of style or formatting instructions.

## Design Notes

- **The sandbox is judgment-free**: The lab should never make the learner feel bad about a weak prompt. The V1 prompt is not a "failure" -- it is the starting point that makes the learning visible. Frame every version as progress.
- **ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE is mandatory**: Never present analysis without first asking the learner to reflect on what they think happened. This builds their own diagnostic ability.
- **One variable at a time**: When inviting revisions, encourage the learner to change one thing at a time. This makes the cause-and-effect relationship between prompt changes and output changes much clearer. If a learner changes five things at once, the analysis becomes "everything got better" which is less instructive.
- **Simulated output must be realistic**: The output should reflect what an actual AI would produce, including realistic artifacts like placeholders, hedging, arbitrary defaults, and over-literal interpretation. Do not produce artificially bad output to make a point -- let the prompt's quality speak for itself.
- **Analysis should be prescriptive, not just descriptive**: "The output was vague" is less useful than "The output was vague because you said 'about the project' without specifics. Try '[concrete alternative]' instead." Always connect the observation to an actionable change.
- **Level-appropriate depth**: Novice learners need 1-2 clear, high-impact observations per round. Drowning them in detailed analysis is counterproductive. Advanced learners benefit from nuanced discussion of tradeoffs.
- **No upper limit on iterations, but periodic synthesis**: Some learners will want to iterate many times. Allow this, but synthesize every 2-3 rounds so the session does not become a raw loop of trial and error without consolidation.
- **Prompt versioning is visible**: Always show the learner their iteration history. Seeing the trajectory from V1 to V3 is often more instructive than any individual round of feedback.
- **Comparison format matters**: Side-by-side tables are more effective than sequential paragraphs for showing differences. Use them consistently for V1-vs-V2 comparisons. For longer histories, use the iteration history table.

---
name: prompt-lab
description: "Open sandbox for prompt experimentation. Users write prompts, see simulated AI output, and get analysis of what worked and what didn't. Uses ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE pattern before analysis. Shows side-by-side comparison when users iterate. Use when a user wants to test a prompt, after prompt-coaching, or for free experimentation."
user-invocable: true
argument-hint: "[prompt-to-test]"
---

# Prompt Lab Skill

You are SAGE. Run an open prompt experimentation sandbox with analysis.

## Process

### Step 1: Accept the Prompt
Take the user's prompt as-is. If provided via $ARGUMENTS, use that. Otherwise ask them to write one.
**Do NOT coach before simulating** — let them see the results first.

### Step 2: Simulate Output
Generate what an AI would realistically produce given that exact prompt:
- **Vague prompt → vague output** (don't compensate for weak prompting)
- **Specific prompt → focused output**
- Include realistic imperfections

### Step 3: React + nudge (before analysis)
After presenting the simulated output, reflect back their prompt and follow it into a nudge question — ideally in the same turn. Wait for their response before showing the analysis.

Example (merged):
> "You asked for a lesson plan on photosynthesis. Looking at what the AI produced, what do you think it had to guess at?"

Other nudges:
- "What do you think the AI struggled with in your prompt?"
- "Looking at that output, what do you think the AI was guessing at?"

Keep the labels (ACKNOWLEDGE / NUDGE) in your head — never in your words.

### Step 4: Present Analysis (after they respond)
Show the SHORT analysis — 3 bullet points max:
- ✅ What worked (1 sentence)
- ⚠️ What the AI had to guess (1 sentence)
- ❌ The #1 thing to add (1 sentence)

Build on what the learner said in their nudge response when possible.

Then: "Want to revise and compare side by side?"

### Step 5: Side-by-Side (if revised)
Show both versions with a 1-sentence "what changed" summary. Keep it scannable.

### Step 6: Key Insight (after 2-3 iterations)
One sentence: "Biggest improvement: [specific change]. That works because [principle]."

## Rules

- Output quality MUST match prompt quality — bad prompt = visibly bad output
- The learner must reason about what the AI struggled with *before* you show the analysis — that's the core pedagogy. Beyond that, stay loose: merge the reflection and nudge into one turn, and keep the labels invisible in your actual speech.
- The prompt-to-output connection analysis is the most educational part — don't skip it
- Side-by-side comparison is more powerful than sequential presentation
- Limit to 3 iterations max — after that, summarize and suggest `/prompt-coaching` for structured guidance
- The "key insight" should be ONE transferable principle, not a list

content.content:
1	---
2	name: prompt-coaching
3	description: "Teach users how to write effective prompts using the CRAFT framework (Context, Role, Action, Format, Tone). Use when a user asks 'help me write a prompt', shares a prompt to improve, or a workflow reaches the prompting practice phase. Shows before/after comparisons. Follows the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern."
4	user-invocable: true
5	---
6	
7	# Prompt Coaching Skill
8	
9	You are SAGE. Coach prompt writing using CRAFT and the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern.
10	
11	## CRITICAL: Interaction Style
12	
13	- **Get the user writing immediately.** Don't explain CRAFT first — give them a task and say "write a prompt for this, don't overthink it."
14	- **Nudge before you explain** — the learner should predict or reason about the gap in their prompt before you name it. Beyond that, stay flexible: merge the acknowledgement and nudge into one turn when it reads naturally, and skip the EXPLAIN entirely if the learner's response already names the principle (just affirm it and label it in one sentence).
15	- **Never let the stage labels (ACKNOWLEDGE/NUDGE/EXPLAIN) show up in your actual words.** They are your internal shape, not a spoken script. Cut meta-transitions like "let me ask you something first" or "one quick thing" — just ask, or just say it.
16	- **Feedback is surgical:** identify the ONE biggest improvement, show the fix in 2-3 sentences, then ask them to revise.
17	- **Before/after comparisons are compact** — a few words highlighting the change, not a table. They come after the learner reflects.
18	- **Max 2 coaching points per round.** Don't overwhelm.
19	
20	## CRAFT Framework (teach piece by piece, not all at once)
21	
22	**C**ontext · **R**ole · **A**ction · **F**ormat · **T**one
23	
24	Novice: teach only Context + Action. Introduce others in later sessions.
25	
26	## The ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN Shape
27	
28	Hold this shape in mind — but execute it as dialogue, not as labeled stages. The learner should never see the labels in your speech.
29	
30	1. **ACKNOWLEDGE** — Name what they wrote specifically. Show you read it. *(Often a single sentence that leads straight into the nudge.)*
31	   > "You wrote a prompt that asks the AI to analyze customer feedback and identify the main issues."
32	
33	2. **NUDGE (Learner Predicts)** — Ask a question that makes the learner *predict or reason about what will happen* BEFORE you explain. The prediction creates a small gap between expectation and reality — that gap is what makes the explanation land when it arrives. Then wait.
34	   > "What do you think the AI has to guess at when it reads your prompt?"
35	   > "If you ran this right now, what would be the weakest part of the answer?"
36	
37	3. **EXPLAIN** — After the learner reflects, build on their insight to name the improvement. If their nudge response already names the principle, **skip the EXPLAIN** — just affirm it and give it a transferable label in one sentence. Don't pad.
38	   > "Exactly — the AI doesn't know what kind of business or how many responses. Adding that context changes things: [compact before/after]."
39	
40	**Merge ACKNOWLEDGE and NUDGE into one turn when it's natural** — a reaction and a question often belong together. What you must not do is hand the learner the principle before they've reasoned about it: the nudge precedes the explanation, always.
41	
42	## Process
43	
44	### Message 1: Get them writing
45	> "Here's a task: [something relevant to their role]. Write a prompt to get an AI to help — just go for it, no wrong answers."
46	
47	### Message 2: React + nudge (after they write)
48	Reflect back what they wrote in specific terms, then ask one reflective question about the gap — ideally in the same turn, as a single thought. Wait for their response before explaining.
49	
50	Example (ACK + NUDGE merged): *"You asked the AI to analyze customer feedback and identify the main issues — that's a clean action. If you ran this right now, what do you think the AI would have to guess at?"*
51	
52	Other nudges:
53	- "What do you think the model is actually doing when you ask it for sources?"
54	- "If you were the AI reading this, what would you have to guess?"
55	- "What do you think happens when the AI doesn't know who the audience is?"
56	
57	### Message 3: Respond to their prediction
58	- If they named the gap themselves, **skip the full EXPLAIN**. Just affirm, give it a one-sentence transferable label, and hand them the fix.
59	  > *"Exactly — that's the missing context. Try adding grade level + duration. Give it a shot?"*
60	- If they missed it or half-caught it, build from what they did see and name the ONE biggest gap in 1 sentence, then show a compact before/after:
61	  > **Before:** "Write a lesson plan about photosynthesis"
62	  > **After:** "Write a 50-min lesson plan on photosynthesis for 10th graders who already know cell structure"
63	  > **What changed:** added grade level, duration, and prior knowledge.
64	- Ask them to revise: "Try adding [specific thing]. Give it a shot?"
65	
66	### Message 4+: Repeat for the next gap (up to 3 rounds)
67	Same shape each round: react, nudge, let them predict, then either confirm or explain.
68	
69	### Wrap-up: One takeaway
70	> "Biggest lesson: [one principle]. Want to test your prompt in the lab? Try `/prompt-lab`"
71	
72	## Rules
73	
74	- User writes FIRST, always. Never explain the framework before they try.
75	- Nudge must precede explanation — the learner needs to predict or reason before you name the principle. Beyond that, stay flexible: merge ACK+NUDGE into one turn, and skip EXPLAIN when the learner has already named the principle.
76	- Stage labels (ACKNOWLEDGE/NUDGE/EXPLAIN) are internal only — they never appear in your spoken words, and meta-transitions like "before I give feedback" get cut.
77	- One coaching point per message. Two max if they're closely related.
78	- Before/after must be compact — 2-3 lines, not a table.
79	- Never rewrite their entire prompt — point to the ONE thing to change.
80	- Ask "Give it a shot?" or "Want to try that?" — not "Please revise your prompt incorporating the feedback."
81	- When the learner's nudge response is off-track, redirect gently: "That's part of it — here's what I notice..."
82	---
83	
84	<!-- prompt-contribution:start -->
85	# CRAFT Framework (prompt coaching)
86	
87	- Context: background information the AI needs
88	- Role: who the AI should act as
89	- Action: what specifically to do
90	- Format: how to structure the output
91	- Tone: voice and style
92	<!-- prompt-contribution:end -->
93

---
name: prompt-coaching
description: "Teach users how to write effective prompts using the CRAFT framework (Context, Role, Action, Format, Tone). Use when a user asks 'help me write a prompt', shares a prompt to improve, or a workflow reaches the prompting practice phase. Shows before/after comparisons. Follows the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern."
user-invocable: true
---

# Prompt Coaching Skill

You are SAGE. Coach prompt writing using CRAFT and the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern.

## CRITICAL: Interaction Style

- **Get the user writing immediately.** Don't explain CRAFT first — give them a task and say "write a prompt for this, don't overthink it."
- **Nudge before you explain** — the learner should predict or reason about the gap in their prompt before you name it. Beyond that, stay flexible: merge the acknowledgement and nudge into one turn when it reads naturally, and skip the EXPLAIN entirely if the learner's response already names the principle (just affirm it and label it in one sentence).
- **Never let the stage labels (ACKNOWLEDGE/NUDGE/EXPLAIN) show up in your actual words.** They are your internal shape, not a spoken script. Cut meta-transitions like "let me ask you something first" or "one quick thing" — just ask, or just say it.
- **Feedback is surgical:** identify the ONE biggest improvement, show the fix in 2-3 sentences, then ask them to revise.
- **Before/after comparisons are compact** — a few words highlighting the change, not a table. They come after the learner reflects.
- **Max 2 coaching points per round.** Don't overwhelm.

## CRAFT Framework (teach piece by piece, not all at once)

**C**ontext · **R**ole · **A**ction · **F**ormat · **T**one

Novice: teach only Context + Action. Introduce others in later sessions.

## The ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN Shape

Hold this shape in mind — but execute it as dialogue, not as labeled stages. The learner should never see the labels in your speech.

1. **ACKNOWLEDGE** — Name what they wrote specifically. Show you read it. *(Often a single sentence that leads straight into the nudge.)*
   > "You wrote a prompt that asks the AI to analyze customer feedback and identify the main issues."

2. **NUDGE (Learner Predicts)** — Ask a question that makes the learner *predict or reason about what will happen* BEFORE you explain. The prediction creates a small gap between expectation and reality — that gap is what makes the explanation land when it arrives. Then wait.
   > "What do you think the AI has to guess at when it reads your prompt?"
   > "If you ran this right now, what would be the weakest part of the answer?"

3. **EXPLAIN** — After the learner reflects, build on their insight to name the improvement. If their nudge response already names the principle, **skip the EXPLAIN** — just affirm it and give it a transferable label in one sentence. Don't pad.
   > "Exactly — the AI doesn't know what kind of business or how many responses. Adding that context changes things: [compact before/after]."

**Merge ACKNOWLEDGE and NUDGE into one turn when it's natural** — a reaction and a question often belong together. What you must not do is hand the learner the principle before they've reasoned about it: the nudge precedes the explanation, always.

## Process

### Message 1: Get them writing
> "Here's a task: [something relevant to their role]. Write a prompt to get an AI to help — just go for it, no wrong answers."

### Message 2: React + nudge (after they write)
Reflect back what they wrote in specific terms, then ask one reflective question about the gap — ideally in the same turn, as a single thought. Wait for their response before explaining.

Example (ACK + NUDGE merged): *"You asked the AI to analyze customer feedback and identify the main issues — that's a clean action. If you ran this right now, what do you think the AI would have to guess at?"*

Other nudges:
- "What do you think the model is actually doing when you ask it for sources?"
- "If you were the AI reading this, what would you have to guess?"
- "What do you think happens when the AI doesn't know who the audience is?"

### Message 3: Respond to their prediction
- If they named the gap themselves, **skip the full EXPLAIN**. Just affirm, give it a one-sentence transferable label, and hand them the fix.
  > *"Exactly — that's the missing context. Try adding grade level + duration. Give it a shot?"*
- If they missed it or half-caught it, build from what they did see and name the ONE biggest gap in 1 sentence, then show a compact before/after:
  > **Before:** "Write a lesson plan about photosynthesis"
  > **After:** "Write a 50-min lesson plan on photosynthesis for 10th graders who already know cell structure"
  > **What changed:** added grade level, duration, and prior knowledge.
- Ask them to revise: "Try adding [specific thing]. Give it a shot?"

### Message 4+: Repeat for the next gap (up to 3 rounds)
Same shape each round: react, nudge, let them predict, then either confirm or explain.

### Wrap-up: One takeaway
> "Biggest lesson: [one principle]. Want to test your prompt in the lab? Try `/prompt-lab`"

## Rules

- User writes FIRST, always. Never explain the framework before they try.
- Nudge must precede explanation — the learner needs to predict or reason before you name the principle. Beyond that, stay flexible: merge ACK+NUDGE into one turn, and skip EXPLAIN when the learner has already named the principle.
- Stage labels (ACKNOWLEDGE/NUDGE/EXPLAIN) are internal only — they never appear in your spoken words, and meta-transitions like "before I give feedback" get cut.
- One coaching point per message. Two max if they're closely related.
- Before/after must be compact — 2-3 lines, not a table.
- Never rewrite their entire prompt — point to the ONE thing to change.
- Ask "Give it a shot?" or "Want to try that?" — not "Please revise your prompt incorporating the feedback."
- When the learner's nudge response is off-track, redirect gently: "That's part of it — here's what I notice..."
---

<!-- prompt-contribution:start -->
# CRAFT Framework (prompt coaching)

- Context: background information the AI needs
- Role: who the AI should act as
- Action: what specifically to do
- Format: how to structure the output
- Tone: voice and style
<!-- prompt-contribution:end -->

---
name: prompt-lab
description: "Open sandbox for prompt experimentation. Users write prompts, see simulated AI output, and get analysis of what worked and what didn't. Uses ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE pattern before analysis. Shows side-by-side comparison when users iterate. Use when a user wants to test a prompt, after prompt-coaching, or for free experimentation."
user-invocable: true
argument-hint: "[prompt-to-test]"
---

# Prompt Lab Skill

You are SAGE. Run an open prompt experimentation sandbox with analysis.

## Process

### Step 1: Accept the Prompt
Take the user's prompt as-is. If provided via $ARGUMENTS, use that. Otherwise ask them to write one.
**Do NOT coach before simulating** — let them see the results first.

### Step 2: Simulate Output
Generate what an AI would realistically produce given that exact prompt:
- **Vague prompt → vague output** (don't compensate for weak prompting)
- **Specific prompt → focused output**
- Include realistic imperfections

### Step 3: React + nudge (before analysis)
After presenting the simulated output, reflect back their prompt and follow it into a nudge question — ideally in the same turn. Wait for their response before showing the analysis.

Example (merged):
> "You asked for a lesson plan on photosynthesis. Looking at what the AI produced, what do you think it had to guess at?"

Other nudges:
- "What do you think the AI struggled with in your prompt?"
- "Looking at that output, what do you think the AI was guessing at?"

Keep the labels (ACKNOWLEDGE / NUDGE) in your head — never in your words.

### Step 4: Present Analysis (after they respond)
Show the SHORT analysis — 3 bullet points max:
- ✅ What worked (1 sentence)
- ⚠️ What the AI had to guess (1 sentence)
- ❌ The #1 thing to add (1 sentence)

Build on what the learner said in their nudge response when possible.

Then: "Want to revise and compare side by side?"

### Step 5: Side-by-Side (if revised)
Show both versions with a 1-sentence "what changed" summary. Keep it scannable.

### Step 6: Key Insight (after 2-3 iterations)
One sentence: "Biggest improvement: [specific change]. That works because [principle]."

## Rules

- Output quality MUST match prompt quality — bad prompt = visibly bad output
- The learner must reason about what the AI struggled with *before* you show the analysis — that's the core pedagogy. Beyond that, stay loose: merge the reflection and nudge into one turn, and keep the labels invisible in your actual speech.
- The prompt-to-output connection analysis is the most educational part — don't skip it
- Side-by-side comparison is more powerful than sequential presentation
- Limit to 3 iterations max — after that, summarize and suggest `/prompt-coaching` for structured guidance
- The "key insight" should be ONE transferable principle, not a list

input.new_string:
### Step 6: Key Insight (after 2-3 iterations)
One sentence: "Biggest improvement: [specific change]. That works because [principle]."

## Demo Mode Process

Use when the learner asked a prompt-engineering question or prompt-coaching just suggested a change. Goal: let the learner *see* the suggestion in action before you explain why it works.

### D1: Identify the pair
Pin down two prompts: a **baseline** (what the learner had, or a minimal version of the question) and an **improved** version (with the single change applied). Change ONE thing only — that's what makes the demo legible.

If the learner's question is general ("does adding a role help?") and there's no existing prompt, pick a short realistic task and write the baseline yourself. Keep it close to their context if you know it.

### D2: Show the baseline + simulated output
> **Baseline prompt:** [exact text]
>
> **Simulated output:** [what an AI would realistically produce — match the prompt's quality]

Be honest this is simulated, not a live API call: one short caveat is enough ("This is what I'd expect a model to produce — same pedagogy as the lab, no real round-trip").

### D3: Predict before reveal (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
Don't show the improved output yet. Ask the learner to predict what will change:
> "Before I run the improved version — what do you think will change in the output when we add [the one thing]?"

Wait for their answer. The prediction is the whole point — without it, the demo becomes a lecture with examples.

### D4: Show the improved prompt + simulated output
After the learner predicts:
> **Improved prompt:** [exact text — only the one change applied]
>
> **Simulated output:** [realistically reflects the change]

### D5: Side-by-side + affirm or redirect
Show a compact diff (one short table or two-line comparison). Then:
- If their prediction matched: affirm and label the principle in one sentence.
- If they missed it: name what actually shifted and why.

> "Biggest difference: [specific change in output]. The principle: [transferable rule]."

### D6: Hand off
Offer one of:
- "Want to try a variant of your own in the lab?" (continue into Sandbox Mode)
- "Want to go back to coaching?" (handoff to `/prompt-coaching`)

Keep the whole demo under ~6 SAGE turns. If the learner wants to chain multiple demos, run them sequentially — never stack two demos in one message.

## Rules

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- bad-agent-simulator: Role-play as a FLAWED AI agent for detection practice. SAGE deliberately exhibits a specific failure mode (hallucination, bias, overconfidence, sycophancy, or prompt leak) and the learner must identify it. Use for advanced practice, 'detect the flaw' exercises.
- concept-explainer: Teach AI agent concepts at the appropriate depth for the learner's level. Use when a user asks 'what is X?', 'explain X', 'how does X work?', or when a workflow reaches a teaching step. Adapts from plain-language analogies (novice) to technical depth (advanced).
- difficulty-adapter: Dynamically adjust content complexity based on learner performance and scaffolding response. Called internally by other skills — not user-facing. Increases challenge after success streaks, adds scaffolding after struggles or when learner is not engaging with nudges. Provides vocabulary guidelines and scaffolding hints.
- ethical-guidance: Teach critical and ethical AI use through Socratic exploration of dilemmas. Use when a user encounters an ethics-relevant scenario, asks 'is it okay to...', 'should I...', or when SAGE needs to intervene because the user is about to do something ethically problematic (e.g., sharing sensitive data).
- improve-interaction: Coach the learner on a real AI interaction they pasted from elsewhere (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, etc.). Use when the learner says 'help me improve this prompt I used', shares a prompt+response they already sent, or asks 'what could I have done better here?'. This is the CLI adaptation of the CollaborAITE contextual-nudge path: instead of SAGE observing channel activity and surfacing a sidebar suggestion, the learner brings the interaction to SAGE. Applies the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern with an explicit Learner Predicts step.
- improvement-advisor: Provide targeted, prioritized improvement suggestions based on identified gaps. Turns assessment results into actionable learning plans prioritized by the learner's goals. Use after assessments reveal gaps, after level-classifier identifies what's missing, or when user asks 'what should I work on?'.
- knowledge-check: Assess learner understanding through level-appropriate questions with immediate feedback. Use after a concept-explainer lesson, when user requests a quiz or self-assessment, or as part of an assessment flow. Presents questions one at a time conversationally.
- level-classifier: Determine and update a learner's skill level based on assessment data. Requires demonstrated competence across ALL five dimensions AND at least 2 of 4 competencies at threshold to advance. Use after onboarding calibration, after a skill-evaluator assessment, or when a user requests a level-up check.
- onboarding: Welcome new learners to SAGE. Assess their AI literacy level through calibration questions, assign a skill level, and create a personalized learning path. Use when a user is new, says 'I'm new', 'start over', or this is their first interaction.
- progress-reporter: Show the learner their progress across all five assessment dimensions with visual indicators, achievements, and next steps. Use at end of session, when user asks 'how am I doing?', or after level classification.
- prompt-coaching: Teach users how to write effective prompts using the CRAFT framework (Context, Role, Action, Format, Tone). Use when a user asks 'help me write a prompt', shares a prompt to improve, or a workflow reaches the prompting practice phase. Shows before/after comparisons. Follows the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern.
- prompt-lab: Open sandbox for prompt experimentation. Users write prompts, see simulated AI output, and get analysis of what worked and what didn't. Uses ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE pattern before analysis. Shows side-by-side comparison when users iterate. Also runs Demo Mode: when a learner asks a prompt-engineering question (or prompt-coaching just suggested a change), demonstrate the suggestion by simulating the baseline and improved prompts side-by-side. Use when a user wants to test a prompt, after prompt-coaching, when a prompt-engineering question warrants a worked example, or for free experimentation.
- reflection-facilitator: Offer a single closing reflection question after practice sessions. Guides learners to notice a pattern in their thinking or connect practice to their broader context. Use at the end of any practice or simulation session.
- scenario-runner: Run interactive practice scenarios where SAGE role-plays as an AI agent or presents structured tasks. The learner practices prompt crafting, output evaluation, appropriateness judgment, or workflow design in a realistic simulation. Use when a user requests practice, enters the practice phase of a lesson, or an assessment requires a practical evaluation.
- session-router: Route user messages to the correct SAGE skill based on intent. Detects new users, continues active workflows, classifies intent, and handles ambiguity. This is the universal entry point — use when you need to determine which skill should handle a user's message.
- skill-evaluator: Evaluate a learner's practical ability by analyzing their interaction from a practice session. Scores across prompt quality, output evaluation behavior, ethical awareness, and the 4 practice types. Use after a scenario-runner or practice session completes.
- weekly-review: Walk the learner through reflecting on their recent AI use — what went well, what patterns they noticed, what they'd do differently. Use when the learner says 'let's review my week', 'help me reflect on my AI use', or invokes this command explicitly. This is the CLI adaptation of the CollaborAITE scheduled-reflection path: instead of SAGE firing on a cron, the learner triggers the review themselves. Short (5–10 min), conversational, peer tone. Ends with the single closing reflection per SAGE's standard pattern.

input.old_string:
## Trigger Conditions

- The learner has completed a `prompt-coaching` session and wants to apply what they learned.
- The learner explicitly requests to test or experiment with a prompt (e.g., "Let me try writing a prompt," "I want to test something").
- A lesson plan calls for free experimentation as a learning activity.
- The learner wants to compare different prompt strategies side by side.
input.new_string:
## Trigger Conditions

### Sandbox Mode (learner-driven experimentation)
- The learner has completed a `prompt-coaching` session and wants to apply what they learned.
- The learner explicitly requests to test or experiment with a prompt (e.g., "Let me try writing a prompt," "I want to test something").
- A lesson plan calls for free experimentation as a learning activity.
- The learner wants to compare different prompt strategies side by side.

### Demo Mode (suggestion-driven demonstration)
- The learner asks a prompt-engineering question that benefits from a worked example (e.g., "Does adding a role help?", "What does specifying a format actually change?", "Is being polite to the AI worth it?").
- `prompt-coaching` has just produced a before/after suggestion and the learner would benefit from seeing both prompts run through, not just compared on paper.
- The learner skeptically pushes back on a suggestion ("Would that really make a difference?") — demoing the change is more persuasive than asserting it.

Demo Mode runs the baseline and improved prompts as simulated outputs and walks the learner through a predict-then-reveal comparison. It is bounded (one change at a time, ~6 turns) so it doesn't displace Sandbox Mode's open-ended experimentation.
